1: Eliphaz challenges Job to call out to any of the holy ones. No mediator is available to him — a lack the book will resolve only in ch. 19 where Job cries for his gōʾēl and ch. 33 where Elihu names the heavenly meliṣ. Christ is the one Mediator who answers every cry (1 Tim Job 2:5).
2: Vexation kills the fool and jealousy slays the simple. Eliphaz’s wisdom tradition locates the source of suffering in the sufferer. Christ absorbs vexation from without, bearing the contradiction of sinners (Heb Job 12:3), demonstrating that affliction is not always self-generated.
3: Eliphaz’s cursed-root image anticipates the fig-tree judgment (Matt Job 21:19) and John Job 15:6 (branches that do not abide are thrown away). The righteous flourishing instead of the wicked finds its ground only in union with Christ the true Vine.
4: The sons of the fool are crushed in court with no defender. Christ’s redemptive work is precisely that of the advocate in the gate (1 John Job 2:1; Ps Job 82:3–4), lifting up the defenseless who have no human rescuer.
5: The harvest devoured by the hungry, wealth taken by thorns. The imagery of cursed ground and lost harvest traces to Gen Job 3:17–18. Christ redeems the curse (Gal Job 3:13), restoring the creation’s fruitfulness in the kingdom he inaugurates.
6: Affliction does not spring from the dust nor does trouble sprout from the ground. Eliphaz’s point cuts both ways: suffering has a cause, but in Christ’s case that cause is entirely outside himself — he bears what he did not sow (Ps Job 69:4).
7: “Man is born to trouble as the sparks fly upward.” The Incarnation is God entering the trajectory of human suffering. Isaiah 53:3 calls the Servant “a man of sorrows and acquainted with grief” — Christ fulfills the destiny of mortal humanity by taking its trouble onto himself completely.
8: Eliphaz counsels seeking God and committing one’s cause to him. This is precisely what Jesus does in Gethsemane and on the cross (Luke Job 23:46; Ps Job 22:1) — the perfect Son who always commits his cause to the Father, even in extremis.
9: God does great and unsearchable things, marvelous without number. Paul cites this doxological register in Rom 11:33 after explaining the mystery of redemption. The unsearchable things find their apex in the cross: “depth of riches both of the wisdom and knowledge of God” (Rom Job 11:33).
10: God sends rain on the earth and water on the fields. Jesus cites this same universal providence in Matt Job 5:45: the Father causes rain to fall on the just and unjust alike. Common grace points toward the greater grace of the gospel offered to all people.
11: He sets on high those who are lowly, and mourners are lifted to safety. Mary’s Magnificat echoes this text (Luke Job 1:52). Christ’s entire ministry enacts this reversal: the Beatitudes, the raising of the lowly, the healing of mourners.
12: God frustrates the devices of the crafty so their hands achieve no success. This divine reversal of human cunning runs through the passion narrative: the schemes of priests and Pharisees to destroy Jesus become the very means God uses to accomplish redemption (Acts Job 2:23).
13: “He catches the wise in their own craftiness” — directly quoted in 1 Cor Job 3:19. Paul applies this to those who boast in human wisdom against the “foolishness” of the cross. The crucified Christ is God’s trap for every wisdom that exalts itself above the knowledge of God (2 Cor Job 10:5).
14: The crafty meet darkness at noon. The crucifixion darkness (Matt Job 27:45; Amos Job 8:9) is the cosmic sign that the world’s wisdom has met its noon-darkness — unable to see the light of the resurrection that follows.
15: God saves the needy from the sword of their mouth and from the hand of the mighty. Christ’s trial before Pilate is the paradigm case: the sword of accusation was at its sharpest, yet God vindicates through resurrection the one who had no earthly defender.
16: The poor have hope, and injustice shuts its mouth. The hope of the poor reaches its foundation in the resurrection of Christ (1 Pet Job 1:3), who is raised as the firstfruits of a new order in which injustice is finally silenced at the judgment.
17: “Blessed is the one whom God reproves; do not despise the Almighty’s discipline.” Cited verbatim in Heb Job 12:5–6 (also Prov Job 3:11–12), applied to Christian suffering as fatherly correction. The logic is Christological: Christ himself “learned obedience through what he suffered” (Heb Job 5:8), modeling the way sonship and suffering coinhere.
18: “He wounds, but he binds up; he shatters, but his hands heal.” Hos Job 6:1–2 draws on this pattern and culminates in “after two days he will revive us; on the third day he will raise us up” — language Paul reads as a resurrection template (1 Cor Job 15:4). Peter quotes the same wound-and-heal dynamic and applies it to Christ’s cross: “by his wounds you have been healed” (1 Pet Job 2:24).
19: Deliverance from six troubles, even seven. The fullness of deliverance Eliphaz sketches (six-then-seven = completeness) is what Christ actually accomplishes: he delivers from every trouble enumerated — death, famine, war, slander, destruction — through his redemptive work.
20: In famine he redeems from death; in war from the power of the sword. The gōʾēl-redemption language used here previews Job’s own cry in 19:25 and finds its referent in Christ who “redeems us from the curse of the law” (Gal Job 3:13) and from the power of death (Heb Job 2:14–15).
21: Hidden from the scourge of the tongue, not afraid of destruction when it comes. The hiddenness of God’s people under trial — Col Job 3:3 (“your life is hidden with Christ in God”) — is grounded in Christ’s own hiddenness in the tomb before the resurrection.
22: Laugh at destruction and famine; not afraid of wild animals. This fearlessness before death finds its warrant only in the resurrection. Paul’s “death is swallowed up in victory — where, O death, is your sting?” (1 Cor Job 15:54–55) is the christological fulfillment of what Eliphaz can only idealize.
23: A covenant with the stones of the field; the wild animals at peace. The Edenic peace Eliphaz promises is the new-creation peace inaugurated by Christ, the second Adam, who restores the harmonious dominion lost in the fall (Isa Job 11:6–9; Rom Job 8:21).
24: Know that your tent is at peace. Christ promises a place prepared (John Job 14:2–3) — the dwelling-place security that Eliphaz dangles conditionally is given as gift to those united to Christ.
25: Offspring will be many; descendants as the grass of the earth. The Abrahamic promise of seed (Gen Job 17:6) is fulfilled in Christ who becomes the ancestor of “many children” (Heb Job 2:10) through his redemptive work: “he shall see his offspring” (Isa Job 53:10).
26: Come to the grave in ripe old age, like a shock of grain. The resurrection of the body grants what Eliphaz could only imagine — not escape from death but its transformation into harvest (1 Cor Job 15:37–38, 42–44, the grain metaphor).
27: “This we have searched out; it is true.” Eliphaz’s confidence in inherited tradition is real but incomplete: it takes the revelation of the cross to make full sense of the wound-that-heals pattern he has rightly observed. The gospel is the deeper logic beneath the wisdom tradition.
